contraventionDescription: Parked in a loading gap marked by a yellow line between two spaces – Contravention Code 20

sections.heading: How to have your penalty charge notice (PCN) for this parking contravention cancelled.
sections.content: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.
sections.contentHtml: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.

sections.heading: Follow our 3 stage process:
sections.content:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)
This may also include diagrams and pictures showing signage requirements etc.
2 Check: General grounds for appeal
Applies to most contraventions.
3 Check: Summarised parking adjudicators decisions
Includes details of example cases won.
sections.contentHtml:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)
This may also include diagrams and pictures showing signage requirements etc.

2 Check: General grounds for appeal
Applies to most contraventions.

3 Check: Summarised parking adjudicators decisions
Includes details of example cases won.

sections.heading: Specific grounds of appeal for this contravention
sections.content: This is a yellow line between two parking spaces. The idea being that during controlled hours only Loading/unloading can take place and outside of controlled hours you can park just like any other yellow line. Some loading/unloading gaps have double yellow lines meaning that you cannot park at any time. The grounds for appeal are the same as for contravention code 01.
sections.contentHtml: This is a yellow line between two parking spaces. The idea being that during controlled hours only Loading/unloading can take place and outside of controlled hours you can park just like any other yellow line. Some loading/unloading gaps have double yellow lines meaning that you cannot park at any time. The grounds for appeal are the same as for contravention code 01.

sections.heading: Signs and lines
sections.content:
This section is very important. We can’t emphasise enough how often the signage is incorrect or indeed there is no signage whatsoever. Road markings can conflict with the signs and quite often the road markings are not clearly visible. If the signs or lines are wrong then you will have grounds to have your parking ticket (penalty charge notice) cancelled.
The rules on signage and lineage on yellow lines are actually quite simple and we set them out below together with diagrams.
Below we show diagrams of the signs and lines which must be in situ by law and much more information that will help you decide whether to appeal against your penalty charge notice.
Double yellow lines
–The double yellow lines must be clear and have a “T” bar at each end so a motorist can clearly see where they start and end. Unless otherwise indicated by signs double yellow lines mean no waiting at any time. There is no longer any requirement for an enforcing authority to place yellow “at any time” signs adjacent to double yellow lines.
Single yellow lines –The lines must be clear and have “T” bars at the end showing where they start and end.  Also were other road markings visible at the point where you parked for example, were there markings from a previous parking bay visible? Or were the number of road markings confusing? Obviously if there were other markings visible for example a previous parking bay then you should appeal.
There must be clear roadside signage at the point of parking showing the hours that they are operational (enforced). These signs must have the “no waiting” symbol and the times and days of enforcement clearly shown. They must be yellow (not white) and the writing in black. They cannot be enforced 24 hours as then double yellow lines would be required. The signs should be at maximum intervals of 30 metres and on every section of yellow line. If there is a break in the yellow line such as for a parking bay or a driveway then there must be signs on each section of the line.
However there is a dispensation for local authorities not to put signs on single yellow lines where they are located within a compliant controlled parking zone. (CPZ)

Controlled Parking Zones Signs
You can obtain maps showing the borders of a controlled parking zone from the enforcing authority and often from their websites.
A Controlled Parking Zone (CPZ) is a designated area (usually a town centre) which has the same enforcement hours on single yellow lines and normally chargeable hours on pay and display bays within the zone. If the hours of enforcement are different on some single yellow lines to the CPZ times then there must be clear roadside signage showing this. However there are strict signage requirements and CPZ signs must be erected at all entry points to the zone and a zone itself should not really be larger than 12 streets. See Statutory Guidance – Controlled Parking Zones (CPZs)
If you are in a Controlled Parking Zone (CPZ), on entering the zone, were the correct signs located at each entry point? (Quite often they are not) They should not be located close to a junction where your attention could be elsewhere. Were they facing the right direction?  Were they clearly visible from your car. (ie. Not obstructed, defaced etc). If not, unless there is clear road-side signage indicating parking restrictions on each section of single yellow line as stated above and within a maximum of 15 metres (3 car lengths) of where you actually parked on the same stretch of yellow line, your PCN should be cancelled as the contravention did not occur. Local authorities must use correct signage for their controlled parking zones otherwise the controlled parking zone is unenforceable (except sections of the roads where there is clear road side signage)
Also in London there are many Red routes. These are not enforced by the local councils rather by transport for London. Interestingly therefore a controlled parking zone cannot have a red route within it. There must be separate zones each side of a red route and therefore controlled zone signs on every road off of every red route. See Red Routes
It is unlawful for a local authority to issue Penalty charge notices in a CPZ when the CPZ signs are not compliant.
Below we show the signs and lines which must be in situ.
It is important to note the information in the boxes immediately underneath each picture as this shows the circumstance in which the sign can be used and also the permitted variations to it. If the sign where you parked is different to the one below and is not a permitted variant your parking ticket could be cancelled.
 
Below: This sign is usually used on double yellow lines in tourist areas such as the seaside when double yellow lines only apply for part of each year.
 

 
Below: This sign is for use on single yellow lines and must be placed at intervals of no more that 15 metres and on every stretch of single yellow line unless the line is in a properly marked and valid controlled parking zone.

 
Below: These are the controlled parking zone signs that must be placed at each entry point to a controlled parking zone. Please also remember that a controlled parking zone should not be more than about a dozen streets in total as stated in the statutory guidance. At the end of each controlled parking zone the “Zone ends” signs should be erected. Without this how would you know that you have or indeed have not left the zone area.

 

 
Controlled Parking Zones Lines
Road Markings must be present, clear, unobstructed by leaves etc and of the correct width in order for any restrictions to be enforceable.
Please note the information regarding sizes and the fact that no variants are permitted.
 

 

 
Controlled Parking Zones Loading
You can park on a single yellow line if you are loading and if loading is not prohibited. (please see the contravention code 2 loading section).
Some local authorities allow a grace period on a single yellow line. You should check with them to make sure that you were allowed the grace period.
There have been many adjudicators’ decisions on loading. See adjudicators decisions on loading.
Remember that you may cite compassionate grounds in any appeal or representation.
Back to Parking

Below we show the signs and lines which must be in situ.

It is important to note the information in the boxes immediately underneath each picture as this shows the circumstance in which the sign can be used and also the permitted variations to it. If the sign where you parked is different to the one below and is not a permitted variant your parking ticket could be cancelled.

 

Below: This sign is usually used on double yellow lines in tourist areas such as the seaside when double yellow lines only apply for part of each year.

 

 

Below: This sign is for use on single yellow lines and must be placed at intervals of no more that 15 metres and on every stretch of single yellow line unless the line is in a properly marked and valid controlled parking zone.

 

Below: These are the controlled parking zone signs that must be placed at each entry point to a controlled parking zone. At the end of each controlled parking zone the “Zone ends” signs should be erected. Without this how would you know that you have or indeed have not left the zone area.

Road Markings must be present, clear, unobstructed by leaves etc and of the correct width in order for any restrictions to be enforceable.

Please note the information regarding sizes and the fact that no variants are permitted.

 

 

 

Controlled Parking Zones Loading

You can park on a single yellow line if you are loading and if loading is not prohibited. (please see the contravention code 2 loading section).

Some local authorities allow a grace period on a single yellow line. You should check with them to make sure that you were allowed the grace period.

There have been many adjudicators’ decisions on loading. See adjudicators decisions on loading.

Remember that you may cite compassionate grounds in any appeal or representation. 

Back to Parking

sections.heading: Controlled Parking Zones Loading
sections.content:
You can park on a single yellow line if you are loading and if loading is not prohibited. (please see the contravention code 2 loading section).
Some local authorities allow a grace period on a single yellow line. You should check with them to make sure that you were allowed the grace period.
There have been many adjudicators’ decisions on loading. See adjudicators decisions on loading.
Remember that you may cite compassionate grounds in any appeal or representation.
